coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted fabrication of copper-functionalized carbon quantum dots for sensitive detection of histidine
coredata.dc:creator: Che, Yucan
Pang, Hongyu
Li, Huiyu
Yang, Lei
Fu, Xinyao
Liu, Shiqi
Ding, Lan
Hou, Juan
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots are emerging as a new class of fluorescent probes due to their unique optical properties. Herein, we presented a simple, one-pot and effective preparation strategy for copper-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Cu-CDs). The Cu-CDs demonstrated excitation-independent emissions, high fluorescent quantum yield (~ 24.4%) and excellent fluorescent stability. The fluorescence of Cu-CDs could be quenched efficiently in the presence of histidine (His) due to the coordination occurring between surface Cu and imidazole side chains of His. Thus, the Cu-CDs could be used as an efficient fluorescent probe for His detection. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent signals of Cu-CDs decreased linearly with the concentrations of His over a range of 0.1–15 µM. The detection limit was 30 nM. The proposed method provided obvious advantages of rapid response, convenience, simplicity, high selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, good results also have been obtained for His detection in real biological fluid samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Microwave irradiation
Fluorescence detection
Histidine
Biological samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and composition of the Cu-CDs
Preliminary applications in biological fluid samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, inset: High-resolution TEM image; (b) size distribution; (c) XRD profile; (d) FT-IR spectrum of the Cu-CDs.
XPS spectrum (a) and high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (b), N1s (c) and Cu2p (d) for Cu-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation (λem = 448 nm) and emission (λex = 340 nm) spectra of Cu-CDs. The inset displays the photograph of the as-prepared Cu-CDs aqueous solution under 365 nm UV illunination; (b) fluorescence spectra of Cu-CDs at different λex from 300 to 400 nm.

(a) FL intensity of the Cu-CDs dispersed in different pH solutions (n = 3); (b) pH effect on the Cu-CDs quenching performance by the same concentration of His and Fe3+ (n = 3); (c) buffer type evaluation at pH 9 (n = 3); (d) optimization of reaction time between His and Cu-CDs.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the Cu-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of His. The curves a–n represent the concentrations of His of 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0, 7.5, 10, 12.5, 15, 20, 25 and 30 μmol L−1, respectively. The inset shows the relationship between F/F0 value and the concentration of His (F and F0 represent the FL intensities of Cu-CDs in the presence and absence of His).

FL intensities of the Cu-CDs solutions containing different kinds of metal ions [Mn+] = 50 μM (a) and other physiological molecules (the concentration of physiological molecules and amino acids is 15 μM) (b).
The schematic illustration of Cu-CDs preparation and histidine detection process.
Recovery (%) (n = 3) of spiked His in diluted human serum samples (50-fold) and urine samples (100-fold).

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted fabrication of copper-functionalized carbon quantum dots for sensitive detection of histidine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Northeast Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots are emerging as a new class of fluorescent probes due to their unique optical properties. Herein, we presented a simple, one-pot and effective preparation strategy for copper-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Cu-CDs). The Cu-CDs demonstrated excitation-independent emissions, high fluorescent quantum yield (~ 24.4%) and excellent fluorescent stability. The fluorescence of Cu-CDs could be quenched efficiently in the presence of histidine (His) due to the coordination occurring between surface Cu and imidazole side chains of His. Thus, the Cu-CDs could be used as an efficient fluorescent probe for His detection. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescent signals of Cu-CDs decreased linearly with the concentrations of His over a range of 0.1–15 µM. The detection limit was 30 nM. The proposed method provided obvious advantages of rapid response, convenience, simplicity, high selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, good results also have been obtained for His detection in real biological fluid samples.

Copper-functionalized carbon dots were fabricated through a one-pot microwave-assisted method and then applied to the sensitive and selective detection of histidine.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: There are 20 amino acids available in nature. Among them, histidine (His) plays a vital role in tissue growth or repair of human and other mammals [1,2]. Its imidazole side chains usually act as the common coordinating ligands in metalloproteins and take part in the catalytic activities of certain enzymes [3–5]. His also presents in proteins as the neurotransmitter or neuromodulator to control the transmission of metal elements in mammalian central nervous system [6]. Studies have proved that low levels of His in plasma may be implicated in many diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, epilepsy, malnutrition and pulmonary disease. While the overexpression of His in physiological fluids, including serum and urine, can also lead to metabolic disorders [7,8]. Therefore, rapid and accurate quantification of His is of great importance in biochemical and clinical applications.

Up to now, several analytical methods for His determination have been established in previous literatures, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9,10], electrochemistry [11,12], capillary electrophoresis [13,14], mass-spectrometry [15] and spectrophotometry [16]. In most cases, these methods often suffer from some inherent drawbacks, including expensive instrumentation, long-time consumption, complicated sample pretreatment procedures and low throughput, which greatly limit their further applications. Compared to other techniques, fluorescent probes are proved to be more favorable for His detection owing to the operational simplicity, real-time detection, good selectivity and high sensitivity [6]. Many fluorescent probes, such as DNA [2], Ag/Au nanoclusters [3,8], organic molecules [5] and semiconductor quantum dots [7,17], have been developed for the sensitive detection of His. Although promising, potential toxicity, multistep chemical modifications and expensive raw materials result in new concerns, such as high synthetic cost, operational complexity and long assay time. So it is still necessary and important to explore facile strategy for selective and sensitive detection of His in biological fluid samples.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as newly emerging fluorescent carbon-based nano-materials, have inspired intense research efforts in the past decade [18–21]. CDs are superior to traditional fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in terms of their distinct merits, such as good water solubility, low toxicity, superior chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility and facile preparation. Hence, CDs have been applied in many potential applications including bio-imaging, optoelectronics, photo-catalysis, drug delivery and fluorescence sensing [22]. Recently, CDs have been used as highly efficient fluorescent probes for the detection of ions, small bio-molecules, environmental pollutants, proteins and so on. Of course, metal ions-modulated CDs fluorescent sensors for His detection have also been developed. The fluorescent emissions of CDs could be quenched by metal ions (for example: Cu2+ and Hg2+) via energy of electron transfer firstly. Upon the addition of His, the fluorescence recovered dramatically due to the high specific interaction between metal ion and side chains of His [1,6]. Although the analytical methods exhibited high sensitivity and operability, the leakage of extra heavy metals may bring secondary pollution and the “off-on” process also increases time consumption. Moreover, other amino acids with similar structures may interfere in the detection results when working with biological samples. Therefore, metal-functionalized CDs, as a new generation of sensing materials, have been developed for the efficient, reliable and selective detection of His in real samples.

In this work, Cu2+ functionalized CDs (Cu-CDs) were synthesized by a facile one-pot microwave-assisted method. Cu2+ was modified on the surface of Cu-CDs, which displayed good affinity with imidazole side chains of His ( Scheme 1). The fluorescence of Cu-CDs could be quenched by His linearly, so the Cu-CDs have potential applications in His detection. Moreover, their fluorescent responses towards various representative biological metal ions and other small molecules have also been investigated. The label-free fluorescent probe demonstrated high selectivity to His over other coexisting metal ions and amino acids. Therefore, the Cu-CDs based probe was applied to the facile, inexpensive and selective detection of His in biological fluids. As far as we know, this is the first example to prepare Cu-CDs probe and achieve good detection performance for His. Such a design strategy is promising in extending the metal-functionalized CDs-based sensor to other target analytes.

All the chemicals and solvents used were of analytical grade without any further purification. CuCl2·2H2O and other soluble metal ion salts were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp (Beijing, China). Ammonium citrate was obtained from Tianjin Jinko Institute of Refined Chemical Engineering. Quinine sulfate for fluorescence standard reference (98%) was supplied by Aladdin Ltd (Shanghai, China). Various amino acids were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. High-purity water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system preparation (Millipore, MA, USA).

CuCl2·2H2O (0.85 g) and ammonium citrate (2.43 g) were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water. The solution was stirred for 20 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a microwave digestion tank and heated in the high-pressure microwave oven. The microwave irradiation was carried out with a programmed temperature control as follows: initial from room temperature to 110 °C within 30 min, from 110 °C to 180 °C within 15 min and finally keeping at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to the room temperature naturally, the final products were collected by centrifugation, filtration and dialysis (cutoff M n: 1.0 kDa) for 2 days. The purified Cu-CDs were dispersed in ultrapure water at a concentration of 500 μg mL−1 for the subsequent fluorescent detection experiments.

The morphology of Cu-CDs was observed on a transmission election microscope (TEM) (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Prior to the TEM analysis, the sample was dispersed in high-purity water and then dropped onto a 300-mesh copper grid coated with a thin carbon film. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded on a Shimadzu XRD-6100 spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å), operated at a step size of 0.04° ranging from 5° to 60°. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained using the KBr pellet method on a Nicolet FT-IR 360 spectrometer with the resolution of 4 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was performed on the Thermo Fisher K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence measurements were carried out at room temperature on the Shimadzu RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a xenon lamp. In the whole experiments, a 1 cm path-length quartz cuvette was used, and the slit widths of excitation and emission spectra were both set at 10 nm.

The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared Cu-CDs was measured by the typical procedure using the following equation:
Q
Y
χ
= Q
Y
R
I χ
I R
O
D R
O
D χ
n χ
2
n R
2
Where QY is the fluorescence quantum yield, I represents the measured integrated emission intensity (Emission wavelength: 340 nm), n is the refractive index, and OD is the optical density measured on a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The subscript “R” and “χ” refer to the standard and as-prepared Cu-CDs, respectively. Herein, we chose quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference (QY
R = 54.6%, excited at 340 nm). The QY of Cu-CDs dissolved in distilled water was measured by comparing the integrated fluorescence emission area at 340 nm and the absorbance value (often less than 0.05) with those of quinine sulfate.

A typical procedure for His detection was performed at room temperature in Tris-HCl buffer solutions (pH=9.0) as follows. Different concentrations of His stock solutions were added into Cu-CDs blank solution. Afterwards, the mixture was diluted to 2.0 mL with Tris-HCl buffer, mixed thoroughly and equilibrated for 5 min. The final concentrations of His could be obtained by simple calculation. The fluorescence spectra at λex = 340 nm were recorded in the range of 370–600 nm at room temperature. The slit widths of excitation and emission were both 10 nm.

To explore the selectivity of the proposed method, different metal ions (such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Cd2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co3+, Fe3+, Zn2+) and small bio-molecules (including urea, glucose, uric acid and other amino acids) were also investigated to replace His.

Fresh human serum samples were obtained from two healthy adult volunteers at Third Hospital of Jilin University (Changchun, China), and stored frozen until assay. The whole experiments were carried out in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines. The relevant institutional committees have approved the experiments. Firstly, 1.0 mL serum sample was mixed with 1.0 mL of acetonitrile to precipitate proteins in order to eliminate the interferences and improve the test recoveries [6]. The mixture was under vortex for 3 min and then centrifuged at 6500 rpm for 8 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane and stored at room temperature. Before the fluorescence detection procedures, the filtrates were diluted 50 times with Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 9.0) and incubated for 20 min at room temperature.

The urine samples were collected from two adult volunteers, and diluted 100 times with Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 9.0) before analysis [3,4]. No other pretreatments were needed in the experiment of urine His detection.

Microwave-assisted synthesis is an effective method for CDs preparation. In this work, we proposed a facile one-pot route to fabricate Cu-CDs by using ammonium citrate as the carbon source and CuCl2·2H2O as the copper source. The rich ammonium ions of ammonium citrate have high affinity towards Cu2+, which could assist retaining Cu2+ during the preparation. The synthetic conditions were optimized in detail, including the amount of CuCl2·2H2O, heating temperature and reaction time. The fluorescent quantum yield (QY) was considered to evaluate the Cu-CDs.

The dependence of QY on the amount of copper ions was shown in Fig. S1a. Using quinine sulfate as the standard reference, the QY of CDs without Cu2+ modification was only 16.7%. The addition of Cu2+ could increase the QY gradually and then decrease. The optimum amount of Cu2+ was found to be 0.85 g and the QY of Cu-CDs was calculated to be 24.4%. According to previously published literatures, the functionalization of Cu2+ could introduce more passivated surface defects, which led to the enhancement of fluorescent properties [23]. Moreover, the heating temperature was also a vital factor for the Cu-CDs synthesis (Fig. S1b). Low temperatures were not enough for the carbonization and surface functionalization, while high temperatures would result in over carbonization and decrease the QY [24]. The temperature of 180 °C was selected. From the controlled experiments conducted, the QY enhanced dramatically with the extension of reaction time from 0 to 3 h, and then kept stable with prolonging the reaction time to 5 h (Fig. S1c).

Through the contrasting group data, we prepared the Cu-CDs at 180 °C for 3 h. The products were consistent with different batches under the same preparation conditions. The obtained Cu-CDs showed good water solubility, and no aggregation or precipitation could be observed after storage for 60 days at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and composition of the Cu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The typical TEM image of Cu-CDs is shown in Fig. 1a. The Cu-CDs particles are nearly spherical and display a narrow size distribution ranging from 2 nm to 5 nm with an average size of 3.4 nm (Fig. 1b). It should be noted that the lattice fringes of the Cu-CDs are rather clear in the high-resolution TEM image (HRTEM, inset of Fig. 1a). The lattice spacing of 0.24 nm is close to the (1 1 2) crystal facet of graphite carbon. The XRD pattern in Fig. 1c shows a broad diffraction peak located at around 2θ = 26.21°, which corresponds to a disordered graphitic structure.

The surface functional groups of Cu-CDs were studied by FTIR (Fig. 1d). The wide absorption peaks around 3158 cm−1 and 3012 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups, respectively. Strong absorption at 1675 cm−1 and 1481 cm−1 are assigned to the C˭O and C˭C groups. The mixed peaks at 1250–1400 cm−1 are due to the aromatic nitrogen heterocycles and stretching vibrations of C-O groups. The characteristic peaks around 800–1000 cm−1 represent the presence of Cu-COOH and Cu-NH2 groups.

XPS analysis was carried out to further investigate the composition and surface state of Cu-CDs ( Fig. 2). The survey spectrum in Fig. 2a exhibits four prominent features of C1s, O1s, N1s and Cu2p, with the atomic percentages of 51.44%, 25.67%, 17.80% and 5.09%, respectively. The high-resolution of C1s spectrum shows the presence of C˭C, C-C, C-N/C-O, C˭N and C˭O groups located at 284.57, 285.25, 286.50, 288.27 and 289.02 eV, respectively, which are consistent with the results of FTIR. The typical N1s spectrum exhibits three distinct peaks at 400.17, 401.22 and 401.91 eV, corresponding to the graphic N, O˭C-NH and C-N, respectively. The partial XPS spectrum of Cu2p demonstrates two components at 934.15 and 954.12 eV, which are assigned to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Cu. Thus, the existence of Cu element reveals that Cu has been well functionalized on the surface of Cu-CDs.

The optical properties of Cu-CDs were further investigated, such as UV–vis absorption spectrum, FL spectra, QY and fluorescent stability. As demonstrated in Fig. 3a, the Cu-CDs dispersed in water exhibit strong absorption in the ultraviolet region and extend to the visible region. Two characteristic transition bands can be observed. One located around 242 nm represents the typical absorption of aromatic sp2 domains, which likely originates from the π-π * transitions of polyaromatic chromophores. The other absorption centered at 340 nm may be ascribed to the n-π * transitions, referring to the energy trap stemmed from surface carboxylic groups (C-O or C˭O).

The Cu-CDs solution showed no obvious fluorescence emission when excited at 242 nm, indicating that the inherent conjugated structures were not the efficient fluorescent emission center [25]. As is seen in Fig. 3b, the Cu-CDs solution emits bright blue fluorescence under UV illumination (365 nm). The fluorescent emission wavelength of Cu-CDs is nearly excitation-independent with shifting the excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm. The maximum emission wavelength is located at 448 nm when excited at 340 nm. The full width at half maximum emission peak is about 70 nm. By fitting the fluorescent decay curve with a mono-exponential decay function, the fluorescence lifetime of Cu-CDs was 6.87 ns (Fig. S2), suggesting a relatively uniform fluorescence radiative process [26]. The result is also consistent with the observation of excitation-independent emission. For comparison, we also fabricated CDs by ammonium citrate alone under the same experimental conditions. The CDs also showed good fluorescence properties, but displayed obvious excitation-dependent emissions (Fig. S3). It is generally agreed that the excitation-dependent emissions may be derived from the presence of various sizes and multiple surface trap status of CDs. Thus, it is hypothesized that the introduction of Cu2+ could modify the surface states, leading to non-radiative transitions between the excited electrons. Using quinine sulfate as the standard, the QY of Cu-CDs is about 24.4%, which is also higher than that of CDs without Cu2+ functionalization.

Then, the effects of pH, long-time storage, continuous UV exposure and ionic strength on the fluorescent properties of Cu-CDs have been investigated. As varying pH from 1 to 8, the fluorescent intensity displays an obvious increase. Continually increasing the pH to 14, a gradual decrease in intensity is observed ( Fig. 4). The pH-sensitive feature is generally explained by the reversible protonation of surface functionalized groups in acid and alkaline medium, which is relevant to the fluorescence emission of Cu-CDs. Note that, there is no apparent change in both fluorescence peak positions and intensities after 60 days storage, 90 min continuous UV exposure and at various NaCl concentrations (0–1.0 mol L−1), implying the excellent fluorescent stability of Cu-CDs (Fig. S4).

The as-prepared Cu-CDs exhibited strong blue emission at 448 nm, and the fluorescence could be quenched rapidly in the presence of His. In order to efficiently explore the Cu-CDs-based fluorescent probe for His detection, some analytical parameters were further optimized.

Generally, pH value of the medium can not only influence the fluorescence properties of Cu-CDs, but also affect the efficiency of quenching process by His. The pH effects on the fluorescence quenched efficiency by His was evaluated under λex = 340 nm (Fig. 4b). The quenched efficiencies (Effq) were calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
Eff
q
=
(
F
0
- F
)
/ F
0
where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of Cu-CDs at 448 nm in the absence and presence of His (or other interference), respectively. When the same concentration of His was added, the Effq increased with increasing the pH from 1 to 8 and then decreased slightly from 9 to 14. However, Fe3+ in the solution could also quench the fluorescence of Cu-CDs, which might interfere with the His detection. As evidenced in Fig. 4b, Fe3+ displayed maximum Effq at pH 4, and continuously increasing the pH led to drastic decrease in Effq. At a high pH value of 9, the Effq of Fe3+ was only 0.027, whereas the Effq of His still kept a relatively high value of 0.328. Therefore, in order to eliminate the interference of Fe3+ and obtain better quenching performance, the pH of solution was set at 9.

Once pH 9 was defined as the optimal pH condition, the type of buffer solution was also evaluated, for example boric acid-sodium tetraborate buffer (H3BO3-Na2B4O7), Tris-HCl buffer, glycine-NaOH buffer (Gly-NaOH) and NH3-NH4Cl buffer. As shown in Fig. 4c, Tris-HCl buffer demonstrates higher sensitivity than other types of buffer. Tris-HCl buffer is positively charged and can efficiently aid in the interaction between His and Cu-CDs. High concentrations of Gly-NaOH and NH3-NH4Cl buffers may react with surface Cu of Cu-CDs, which impedes the interaction between Cu-CDs and His and decrease the detection sensitivity. Thus, we chose Tris-HCl buffer system for subsequent fluorescence measurements.

As shown in Fig. 4d, the time-dependence of Cu-CDs towards His was also performed in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9). With the addition of His, the fluorescence of Cu-CDs solution quenched immediately within 0.5 min and the plot leveled off to the minimum value after 4 min, indicating that His completely interacted with the Cu-CDs in 4 min. Therefore, the fluorescence measurements were recorded after adding His for a fixed time of 5 min.

We investigated the feasibility and sensitivity of such Cu-CDs-based probe for quantitative detection of His in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 9.0). The Cu-CDs solution in the absence of His exhibited a strong fluorescence emission at 448 nm when excited at 340 nm. The addition of His led to an obvious decrease in fluorescence intensity, but no obvious shift in fluorescence emission positions could be observed ( Fig. 5). Thus His could effectively quench the fluorescence of Cu-CDs.

To investigate the nature of the quenching process, the fluorescence excited-state lifetime measurements and UV–vis spectra have been carried out. When different concentrations (0–15 μmol L−1) of His were added to the Cu-CDs solution, His could recombine with the surface Cu of Cu-CDs, which led to the fluorescence quenching performance and distinct changes of absorbance at around 340 nm and 540 nm (Fig. S2a). Meanwhile, the maximum excitation wavelength of Cu-CDs aqueous solution is 340 nm. The absorption spectrum of Cu-CDs-His shows quite precise overlapping with the excitation band of Cu-CDs, and the lifetimes of Cu-CDs, Cu-CDs-2 μM His and Cu-CDs-5 μM His were measured to be 6.87 ns, 6.16 ns and 5.89 ns, respectively (Fig. S2b). Therefore, the observed fluorescence quenching performance should be attributed to the competition of external energy.

For the sensitivity study, different concentrations of His ranging from 0 to 30 μM were investigated under the optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 5, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs decreases gradually as the His concentration increases. Based on Stern-Volmer plot analysis, the fluorescence intensity ratios of F/F0 vs the His concentration (0.1–15 μM) can be curve-fitted into the following equation:
F / F
0
= ( 0.9957 ± 0.0038 ) − ( 0.0290 ± 0.0005 )
C
His
,
μ mol L
- 1
(
R
2
= 0.993 )

The correlation coefficient R2 is 0.993 and the detection limit is calculated to be 30 nM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3). To assess the repeatability, eleven batches of Cu-CDs were applied to measure 1 μM of His, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.4%, implying good repeatability of the proposed method. As illustrated in Table S1, the results of our proposed detection method for His are compared with other reported fluorescent detection methods. The proposed method shows superior sensitivity and low detection limit. Moreover, this approach required no expensive equipment, complicated “off-on” process and derivatization, and thus demonstrated great potential in rapid His detection from real samples.

To verify the selectivity of Cu-CDs-based probe to His in practical applications, 14 metal ions and other physiological molecules were used for the comparative experiments under the same detection conditions, including Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Cd2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, urea, glucose, uric acid, cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), phenylalanine (Phe), methionine (Met), isoleucine (Ile), tryptophan (Trp), proline (Pro), lysine (Lys), valine (Val), glutamic (Glu), leucine (Leu), arginine (Arg) and alanine (Ala). As shown in Fig. 6, only His can trigger the immediate and significant quenching of Cu-CDs, whereas other ions or small molecules show negligible quenching effects, indicating that the as-prepared Cu-CDs demonstrate excellent detection selectivity towards His. Additionally, the Cu-CDs-His system was relatively less influenced by co-existing physiological molecules (Fig. S5).

In contrast, CDs were synthesized from ammonium citrate alone in the same manner without the addition of CuCl2·2H2O. The effects of different metal ions and His on the fluorescent intensity of CDs were shown in Fig. S6. Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co3+ and Fe3+ exhibited efficient quenching performance to CDs, but no tremendous fluorescence quenching could be observed by His. Subsequently, we quenched the fluorescence of CDs by Cu2+, and it could be observed that the addition of His could remove Cu2+ from the surface of CDs and recover the fluorescence of CDs. The “off-on” process was different completely from the quenching process of Cu-CDs system induced by His. Therefore, the surface Cu2+ modification can not only efficiently increase the recognition combination due to the side chains of His and Cu2+, but also increase the detection selectivity due to the surface passivation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary applications in biological fluid samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of Cu-CDs-based probe in practical applications has been validated in real human serum and urine samples. The human serum and urine samples were collected from two adult volunteers (1 man and 1 woman). In order to ensure the concentration of His in the linear range and eliminate possible interferences from proteins, appropriate pretreatment and dilution were necessary (see details in Experimental section). The concentrations of His in real samples were determined by the standard addition method and the results are listed in Table 1. Then, recovery tests have been carried out by spiking 1 μM, 2 μM and 5 μM of His to the samples. The concentrations determined in spiked samples by the proposed method were in good agreement with the spiked concentrations. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 3.6% and the recoveries ranged from 96% to 108%. The preliminary investigation demonstrated the potential applicability of the Cu-CDs based fluorescent probe for the detection of His in real biological fluid samples.

In summary, fluorescent Cu-CDs were fabricated by a one-pot microwave-assisted method. The Cu-CDs demonstrated strong blue emission, excellent water solubility and good fluorescent stability. Then, we proposed the analytical conditions and detection rationale of facile, convenient and economical fluorescent detection of His using Cu-CDs as the fluorescent nano-probe. A good linear relationship existed ranging from 0.1 to 15 μM. Compared with conventional fluorescent chemosensors, the proposed method exhibited good sensitivity and selectivity, implying great potential applications in building new strategies for chemical and biological detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (NSFC, No. 21804017), the Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project, China (No. LBH-Z18009) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (No. 2572018BC10).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.12.098
